Projects / The Second Brain: LLM-maintained wiki

The Second Brain: LLM-maintained wiki

Compile knowledge once and keep it current, a deliberate alternative to query-time RAG

Client/Context

Personal project

Role

Knowledge architect & curator (the LLM authors the wiki layer)

Timeline

2026 (~2 months)

Audience

Anyone whose reading outpaces their synthesis

Technologies

Markdown Git Obsidian Claude Code

Agentic toolchain

CLAUDE.md + SCHEMA.md (schema-as-harness) Claude Code CLI: read raw → write wiki → fix cross-refs in one pass One scheduled routine (transcript retry, since disabled)

The problem

RAG and file-upload tools (NotebookLM, ChatGPT uploads) re-derive knowledge from raw chunks on every query. Nothing accumulates, the cross-source synthesis never gets built, and contradictions stay hidden. I was building knowledge over months across engineering work, the 2025-26 AI paradigm shift, CS foundations, and career strategy. That meant paying the synthesis cost repeatedly and losing the connections between sources. The obvious fix, a hand-maintained wiki, dies on its own, because the bookkeeping burden outgrows the value.

Architecture & why

A compiled wiki the LLM maintains: ingest → compile → lint. The model owns all writing and cross-referencing, and a checked-in schema governs how it behaves.

flowchart TD
  R["Raw sources (immutable)<br/>LLM reads, never writes"]
  W["Wiki (LLM-owned)<br/>summaries · entities · concepts · synthesis · index · log"]
  S["Schema: CLAUDE.md + SCHEMA.md<br/>single source of truth for conventions"]
  R -- "Ingest: read → summarise → update pages → append log" --> W
  W -- "Query/Synthesis: read index → drill in → file answers back as pages" --> W
  W -- "Lint: orphans · contradictions · stale stats · link integrity" --> W
  S -. "governs every operation" .-> W
  • Decision: a compiled, LLM-maintained wiki, not query-time RAG. Alternative rejected: embedding retrieval (the NotebookLM / ChatGPT-upload default). Why: RAG rediscovers everything from scratch on every query and leaves no artifact behind. The wiki compiles once and stays current. Cross-references, flagged contradictions, and synthesis are already on the page when you get there. Knowledge becomes a persistent asset instead of a per-query re-derivation.
  • Decision: markdown-in-git + Obsidian as the substrate. Alternative rejected: a hosted SaaS knowledge tool or database-backed app. Why: plain text in git gives me version history, a diffable audit trail, and local-first ownership for free. Obsidian adds graph view and backlinks with no UI to build. Be the customer, not the product.
  • Decision: a hand-curated index.md as the retrieval layer. Alternative rejected: embedding-based search or a vector DB from day one. Why: at ~100 pages, an index file is enough, and it avoids embedding infra, re-indexing cost, and a retrieval black box. I documented it as an explicit “not yet,” not a “never.” Search gets built when scale forces it.
  • Decision: schema-as-config. Alternative rejected: ad-hoc per-session prompting of a generic chatbot. Why: I encoded the rules in config (never modify raw sources · one concept per page · link back to sources · flag contradictions, don’t resolve them · update, don’t duplicate). That turns the LLM into a disciplined maintainer whose behaviour holds across sessions. The config is the single source of truth for how the wiki is built.

Evals / validation

Validation here is knowledge-base health, run as an explicit discipline rather than a code test suite:

  • Lint passes verify zero orphan pages, full index coverage, and frontmatter discipline. They caught and fixed broken wikilinks, a stale source count, and a 0-byte legacy file.
  • Contradiction discipline: contradictions get flagged, not silently resolved. For example, instructional-design frameworks graded “well-evidenced vs. dogma,” and a real deployment-model contradiction worked out on a dedicated synthesis page.
  • A designed falsification protocol borrows five practices from assessment science (predict-before-reading, retrieval-practice quizzing, contradiction sweeps, claim-canary auditing, expert spot-checks). The point is to tell a wiki that teaches understanding apart from one that only performs it.
  • Refactor integrity: raw-source renames verified byte-identical via git diff. A single page rename propagated cleanly across 17 inbound links in 13 files with zero stale references.
  • Honest caveat (found during this writeup): the wiki’s self-reported page counter lagged the real file count (90 vs. 97). That’s exactly the drift the lint pass exists to catch, and the next pass’s first finding.

Outcome

  • Grew from a 19-page seed to 100 markdown pages (97 content + 3 infrastructure) compiled from 66 hand-curated sources across 7 domain clusters. Every page is LLM-authored, 0 written by hand, maintained over ~25 commits across two months.
  • Real maintenance payoff: a single ingest routinely touches 10-15 pages. The largest batch absorbed 20 sources → 26 new pages + 12 updated pages in one compile.
  • ~10,700 lines of interlinked markdown, with the heaviest synthesis pages running 250-385 lines. It’s a living, queryable corpus that compounds instead of decaying.

Personal project. The whole thesis is the division of labour. I own sourcing, curation, the schema design, and the questions. The LLM authored 100% of the wiki prose. The claim here is the system design and curation, not the writing. It’s a months-long personal knowledge system with real architectural decisions, not a shipped product. The agentic footprint is deliberately light (a schema harness plus a read-raw/write-wiki loop), even though the wiki’s content covers heavier primitives.

Impact & results

100
Pages compiled (0 hand-written)
66
Sources ingested & cross-linked
7
Domain clusters maintained
